text: Xylitol is a sugar alcohol widely used as a sweetener in sugar-free products including chewing gum, candies, baked goods, and certain peanut butters. In dogs, xylitol causes profound hypoglycemia through stimulation of insulin release from pancreatic beta cells. Doses as low as 0.1 g/kg can produce hypoglycemia, while doses above 0.5 g/kg are associated with acute hepatic necrosis. Clinical signs develop within 30–60 minutes of ingestion and include vomiting, ataxia, collapse, and seizures. Treatment involves immediate IV dextrose supplementation and hepatoprotective therapy. Prognosis is guarded to poor with hepatotoxic doses.

metadata.title: Xylitol toxicosis in dogs: a retrospective study of 192 cases
metadata.journal: Journal of Veterinary Emergency and Critical Care

text: In the 192 cases reviewed, dogs that ingested xylitol doses between 0.1–0.5 g/kg developed hypoglycemia alone, while those ingesting >0.5 g/kg showed elevated hepatic enzymes (ALT, AST) within 12–24 hours. Hepatic failure was observed in 27 cases (14%). Breeds showing the highest case frequency included Labrador Retrievers, Golden Retrievers, and mixed breeds, likely reflecting the broader population rather than breed-specific susceptibility. Treatment with IV dextrose, N-acetylcysteine, and SAMe supplementation was associated with improved outcomes in hepatotoxic cases. Owner education regarding common xylitol-containing products is critical for prevention.

metadata.title: Xylitol toxicosis in dogs: a retrospective study of 192 cases
metadata.journal: Journal of Veterinary Emergency and Critical Care

text: Theobromine and caffeine, both methylxanthines present in chocolate, are responsible for chocolate toxicosis in dogs and cats. Dark chocolate and baking chocolate contain significantly higher methylxanthine concentrations (14.4–16.3 mg theobromine/g) compared to milk chocolate (1.6–2.1 mg/g). White chocolate contains negligible methylxanthines and poses minimal toxicity risk. Clinical signs include polydipsia, vomiting, diarrhea, restlessness, tachycardia, muscle tremors, and seizures. The toxic dose threshold for theobromine is approximately 20 mg/kg, with potentially fatal doses above 100–200 mg/kg. Cats are metabolically susceptible but rarely ingest chocolate voluntarily.

metadata.title: Methylxanthine toxicosis in companion animals: clinical presentation and management
metadata.journal: Veterinary Clinics of North America: Small Animal Practice

text: Treatment of chocolate toxicosis is primarily supportive. Induction of emesis is recommended within 2 hours of ingestion if the animal is asymptomatic. Activated charcoal (1–3 g/kg) administered orally reduces further methylxanthine absorption. Intravenous fluid therapy corrects dehydration and facilitates renal methylxanthine excretion. Cardiac arrhythmias may require lidocaine or metoprolol. Seizures are managed with diazepam or phenobarbital. Muscle tremors may respond to methocarbamol. Recovery typically occurs within 24–72 hours with appropriate supportive care, though fatalities have been reported in cases with delayed presentation or large ingestion doses.

metadata.title: Methylxanthine toxicosis in companion animals: clinical presentation and management
metadata.journal: Veterinary Clinics of North America: Small Animal Practice

text: Grape and raisin toxicosis in dogs can cause acute kidney injury (AKI), though the precise toxic agent remains unidentified as of the current literature. All forms of grapes and raisins — fresh, dried, seedless, seeded, commercial, or homegrown — have been implicated in toxicosis. The toxic dose has not been established, and idiosyncratic reactions occur; some dogs develop AKI after small amounts while others appear unaffected by larger quantities. Clinical signs include vomiting within 6 hours of ingestion, followed by lethargy, anorexia, abdominal pain, oliguria, and anuria in severe cases. Azotemia develops within 24–72 hours. Currants (Ribes spp.) are also implicated.

metadata.title: Grape and raisin toxicosis in dogs: 43 cases and literature review

text: Allium species toxicosis, caused by ingestion of onions, garlic, leeks, or chives, produces oxidative hemolytic anemia in both dogs and cats. Cats are reportedly 2–3 times more sensitive than dogs due to differences in hemoglobin structure. N-propyl disulfide and other organosulfur compounds oxidize hemoglobin, forming Heinz bodies and methemoglobin, leading to hemolytic anemia. Clinical signs include vomiting, diarrhea, lethargy, pale mucous membranes, tachycardia, and in severe cases, hemoglobinuria. A toxic dose in dogs is approximately 15–30 g/kg of onion, though garlic is approximately 5 times more potent by weight. Cooked forms retain toxicity.

metadata.title: Allium species toxicosis in small animals: mechanisms and clinical management

text: Acetaminophen (paracetamol) toxicosis in cats results in methemoglobinemia, hepatocellular necrosis, and facial/paw edema due to cats' deficient glucuronidation capacity. A single tablet (325–500 mg) can be lethal in cats. In dogs, hepatotoxicity predominates at higher doses (>200 mg/kg), while methemoglobinemia is less common. Clinical signs in cats develop within 1–4 hours and include muddy brown mucous membranes indicating methemoglobin, depression, hypothermia, tachypnea, and facial swelling. N-acetylcysteine is the primary antidote, replenishing glutathione stores. S-adenosylmethionine (SAMe) and ascorbic acid are adjunctive treatments. Prognosis in cats is guarded even with prompt treatment.

metadata.title: Acetaminophen toxicosis in cats and dogs: a comparative review
